coredata.dc:title: Electron scattering of inhomogeneous gap in graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Belokda, Fatima
Jellal, Ahmed
Atmani, El Houssine
coredata.dc:description: We study the scattering of Dirac electrons in circular graphene quantum dots with two different gaps subject to an electrostatic potential. The obtained solutions of the energy spectrum are used to determine the scattering coefficients at the interface of the two regions. Using the asymptotic solutions at large arguments, we explicitly determine the radial component of the reflected current density and the scattering efficiency. It is found that the presence of a mass term outside, in addition to another one inside the quantum dot, strongly affects the scattering of electrons. In particular, a non-null square modulus of the scattering coefficient is found at zero energy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Circular quantum dot
Inhomogeneous gap
Scattering

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast doctopic highlightsabst misctext orcid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (Color online.) Dirac electrons propagating in a gapped graphene subject to the potential barrier in a circular quantum dot of radius r 0. Here the incident and reflected electron waves reside in the conduction band with gap Δ1, while the transmitted wave inside the dot corresponds to a state in the valence band with gap Δ2.

(Color online.) The scattering efficiency Q versus the quantum dot radius r 0 for Δ2 = 0.2 and V = 1. Three regimes of the incident energy are considered (a, d): E < W −, (b, e): W − < E < W +, (c, f): E > W +. Here (a, b, c): Δ1 = 0.5 and (d, e, f): Δ1 = 0.7.
(Color online.) The scattering efficiency Q versus the incident energy E for Δ2 = 0.2 and V = 1. Different sizes of the quantum dot are considered. Here (a, b): Δ1 = 0.5 and (c, d): Δ1 = 0.9.
(Color online.) The scattering efficiency Q versus the incident energy E for Δ2 = 0.2 and r 0 = 3. Different values of the potential V are considered. Here (a): Δ1 = 0.5 and (b): Δ1 = 0.9.

(Color online.) The scattering efficiency Q versus the incident energy E for V = 1 and r 0 = 2. Different values of the gap inside quantum dot Δ2 are considered. Here, (a): Δ1 = 0.5 and (b): Δ1 = 0.9.

(Color online.) The scattering efficiency Q versus the incident energy E for V = 1, r 0 = 2 and Δ2 = 0.2. Different values of the gap outside quantum dot Δ1 are considered. Here, (a): Δ2 = 0.2 and (b): Δ2 = 0.7.

(Color online.) The square modulus of the scattering coefficients |c
m |2 (m = 0,1,2,3) versus the incident energy E for Δ2 = 0.2 and V = 1 with different sizes of quantum dot (a, b): r 0 = 3, (c, d): r 0 = 5, (e, f): r 0 = 7. Here (a,c,e): Δ1 = 0.1 and (b,d, f): Δ1 = 0.5.

(Color online.) The radial component of the reflected density current
j
r
r
versus the incident angle θ for Δ2 = 0.2 and V = 1, (a): E = 0.6 and r 0 = 3.3, (b): E = 0.65 and r 0 = 5, (c): E = 0.69 and r 0 = 7.25, (d): E = 0.35 and r 0 = 8.7. Different values of the gap outside quantum dot Δ1 are considered.

head.ce:title: Electron scattering of inhomogeneous gap in graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Theoretical Physics, Faculty of Sciences, Chouaïb Doukkali University, PO Box 20, 24000 El Jadida, Morocco
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Theoretical Physics, Faculty of Sciences, Chouaïb Doukkali University, PO Box 20, 24000 El Jadida, Morocco

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Nanostructures and Advanced Materials, Mechanics and Thermofluids

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Nanostructures and Advanced Materials, Mechanics and Thermofluids, FST Mohammedia, Hassan II University, Casablanca, Morocco

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We study the scattering of Dirac electrons in circular graphene quantum dots with two different gaps subject to an electrostatic potential. The obtained solutions of the energy spectrum are used to determine the scattering coefficients at the interface of the two regions. Using the asymptotic solutions at large arguments, we explicitly determine the radial component of the reflected current density and the scattering efficiency. It is found that the presence of a mass term outside, in addition to another one inside the quantum dot, strongly affects the scattering of electrons. In particular, a non-null square modulus of the scattering coefficient is found at zero energy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene [1] has incredible transport properties [2,3] letting it to be a potential candidate for technological applications in the future [4]. The interaction of electrons moving around the carbon atoms with the periodic potential of the graphene honeycomb lattice generates relativistic massless Dirac fermions showing a linear energy dispersion [5,6]. These fermions have been found to travel with a speed much faster than that of electrons in semiconductors [7,8]. Additionally, graphene remains capable of conducting electricity even at the limit of nominal carrier concentration, meaning that it never stops conducting. In contrast, Klein tunneling (full transmission) provides a window path for graphene electrons that limits the efficiency of electrostatic confinement [5,9]. Consequently, the fabrication of graphene-based materials will remain a great challenge for various flexible device applications. One way to overcome such situation is to confine the electrons in graphene using different techniques and then the realization of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can offer an alternative solution.

GQDs are made up of a single atomic layer of nano-sized graphite. They have many of the same properties as graphene, including a large surface area, a big diameter, and superior surface grafting employing π − π conjugation and surface groups [10,11]. Recently, QDGs have been extensively discussed both theoretically [12–18] and experimentally [19–21]. Different methods have been proposed to confine electrons in graphene and then generate interesting systems based-graphene. These concern for instance employing thin single-layer graphene strips [12,14] or nonuniform magnetic fields [22]. Another way to use low-disorder graphene crystallographically matched to hexagonal boron nitride substrate and electrostatic confinement [23]. It is found that GQDs strongly depend on their sizes, shapes and nature of edges [6]. GQDs can be applied in the spin qubits and quantum information storage [5,13]. Also they can be used in the fields of bio-imaging, sensors, catalysis, photovoltaic devices, superconductors and so on [24].

On the other hand, systems made of gapped graphene with different band gaps can be used to host topologically protected metallic channels in amazing ways. As a result, due to periodic interlayer interaction with substrates, different band gaps can be generated in graphene [25–27]. For example, a heterostructure comprised of two graphene or graphene-like materials with two different gaps, such as the atomic layers of hybridized BN and graphene domains, could fall into this category [28,29]. In this context, the helical states around a mass-inverted quantum dot in graphene was studied in [30]. To introduce a mass-inverted quantum dot, a heterojunction between two separate mass domains is used in a similar way to the domain wall in bilayer graphene. It was shown that the eigenstates are doubly degenerate, with each state propagating in opposing directions, preserving graphene's time-reversal symmetry.

Motivated by the results mentioned above and especially [30], we study the scattering of electrons in circular graphene quantum dot with two different gaps through an electrostatic potential. We analytically determine the solutions of energy spectrum by solving Dirac equation. In some limit, we explicitly obtain the radial component of density current associated to reflected wave. This is used to compute the corresponding scattering efficiency in terms of the gaps outside and inside the quantum dot. The main characteristics of these quantities are studied in relation to the physical parameters of our system. For this, we identify different scattering regimes as a function of the radius, applied potential, two gaps and incident energy. As results, we show that the energy gap outside the quantum dot strongly affects the scattering of electrons in graphene.

The present paper is organized as follows. In section 2, we set a theoretical model describing our system made of circular graphene quantum dot with two gaps. Subsequently, we establish the spinor solutions of the Dirac equation for the two regions. We use the continuity of the wave functions at the boundary to explicitly determine physical quantities in section 3. We numerically analyze and discuss the main results under various conditions in section 4. Finally, we conclude our work.

To achieve our goal, let us consider graphene quantum dot of radius
r
0
with two mass terms Δ ( r ) and electrostatic potential V ( r ) . Mathematically, we have

To illustrate our results and give a better understanding let us consider the asymptotic behavior of the Hankel function for large argument

It is worth noting that they can be used to find resonances in our system [31]. Actually (33)–(34) show a strong dependence on the energy gap
Δ
1
outside the quantum dot that is not the case for analogue results obtained in [18]. As a consequence, we will study the rule will be played by
Δ
1
to affect the scattering problem of our system.

Let us analyze other physical quantities related to the radial current for the reflected wave (33). Indeed, the scattering cross-section σ is defined by
(35)

r
.

To go deeply into the study of the scattering problem for Dirac electrons in circular graphene quantum dots, we consider the scattering efficiency, Q. To be more specific, Q is used to compare scattering on the quantum dots of various sizes [31]. It is defined by the ratio between the scattering cross-section and the geometric cross-section
(38)
Q =
σ
2
r
0
and then using (37) to get
(39)
Q =
4
r
0
( E +
Δ
1
)
∑
m = 0
∞
|
c
m
|
2
.
We emphasis that in the case
Δ
1
= 0 our results reduce to those obtained in [18]. We will numerically show how the presence of
Δ
1
will affect the scattering problem.

We proceed to a numerical analysis by considering various conditions of the physical parameters to underline the main features of our system. According to the experimental results [32,33], we chose the size of quantum dot to range between zero and 20 nm. Fig. 2 plots scattering efficiency Q versus quantum dot radius
r
0
, for V = 1 and
Δ
2
= 0.2 , with (a, b, c):
Δ
1
= 0.5 and (d, e, f):
Δ
1
= 0.7 . Three scattering regimes of the incident energy E are considered. The results for the regime E <
W
−
outside the quantum dot are shown in Figs. 2(a, d). We notice that Q increases linearly for a small radius, but when the radius reaches a certain value, Q begins to change by showing oscillatory behavior until it reaches its maximum. By increasing further
r
0
, we observe a decrease in the oscillation shape of Q. We notice that Q is very sensitive to the incident energy E because it decreases when E increases. Furthermore, when
r
0
approaches 4, the maximum value for
Δ
1
= 0.5 is Q = 4 , as shown in Fig. 2a, and when
r
0
approaches 8, the maximum value is Q = 3 , as shown in Fig. 2d. The period of oscillations corresponding to
Δ
1
= 0.5 is small compared to the case
Δ
1
= 0.7 . Figs. 2(b, e) depict the behavior of Q for the electronic state inside the quantum dot
W
−
< E <
W
+
. It is clear that Q increases linearly until a certain value of
r
0
, at which point Q changes into an oscillatory shape with varying amplitudes depending on the incident energy E. In fact, for
Δ
1
= 0.5 when
r
0
tends to 6 and for
Δ
1
= 0.7 when
r
0
tends to 10 these amplitudes change inversely with incident energy values, and afterward the oscillations begin to be damped. The final energy E >
W
+
regime is depicted in Figs. 2(c, f). As one sees, for a small radius, Q changes in a linear manner and its values are very close to each other regardless of the incident energy. When the radius reaches a certain value, however, Q changes by oscillating with decreasing amplitudes until a certain
r
0
value is reached. These results demonstrate the effect of the
Δ
1
gap on the scattering efficiency Q.

In Fig. 3 , we plot the scattering efficiency Q as a function of incident energy E for V = 1 ,
Δ
2
= 0.2 , two values of
Δ
1
= 0.5 , 0.9 , and different quantum dot sizes. For small values of
r
0
in Figs. 3(a, c) Q takes 4 as maximum value for E = 0 and
Δ
1
= 0.5 . It rapidly decreases until it approaches zero at E =
Δ
1
= 0.5 . When the energy increases, Q gradually increases by oscillating to reach constant values. When
Δ
1
= 0.9 , we see that Q is minimal at E = 0 , but it reaches its maximum value of 1.2 when E is increased. Later on, it decreases to approaches zero at E =
Δ
1
= 0.9 and after that, both of them increase. For large radius
r
0
, Q is not zero for E = 0 and shows large maxima in Figs. 3(b, d). When
r
0
increases, the maximum value of Q decreases and then approaches zero for E =
Δ
1
= 0.5 . By increasing E, Q takes constant values and strongly depends on
r
0
. In the case of
Δ
1
= 0.9 , Q has a minimum value at E = 0 and a maximum value as E increases. At E =
Δ
1
= 0.9 , it approaches zero, but as E increases, we see an increase in Q for both sizes (
r
0
= 5.75 , 6.25 ).

To demonstrate how the potential V affects scattering, we plot, in Fig. 4 , the scattering efficiency Q as a function of incident energy E for
Δ
2
= 0.2 and
r
0
= 3 , with (a):
Δ
1
= 0.5 and (b):
Δ
1
= 0.9 . It is clearly seen that when E closes to zero, Q is not null and its values strongly depend on both parameters V and
Δ
1
. Moreover, as long as E increases, we observe that Q decreases towards
Δ
1
by showing small oscillations with different amplitudes. Following that, one can see that Q begins to rise by increasing E for a large potential V. An intriguing conclusion is that for E <
Δ
1
= 0.5 in Fig. 4a, Q takes small values compared to E <
Δ
1
= 0.9 in Fig. 4b. However, for the case E >
Δ
1
= 0.5 and E >
Δ
1
= 0.9 we observe the opposite behavior.

For various values of the energy gap
Δ
2
inside the quantum dot with V = 1 and
r
0
= 2 , Q is plotted, in Fig. 5 , as a function of incident energy E. Here we choose two values such that (a):
Δ
1
= 0.5 and (b):
Δ
1
= 0.9 . It is clearly seen that Q is not null for E = 0 . As E increases, Q decreases and approaches zero at the point E =
Δ
1
= 0.5 , as shown in Fig. 5a. Afterward, Q oscillates to reach constant values depending on
Δ
2
. Fig. 5b shows that when
Δ
1
= 0.9 , Q behaves mostly the same regardless of
Δ
2
in the interval 0 < E <
Δ
1
= 0.9 , whereas when E >
Δ
1
= 0.9 , Q oscillates and then increases.

Fig. 6 depicts the effect of the gap outside the quantum dot
Δ
1
on scattering efficiency Q as a function of incident energy E. It is clear that Q behaves differently than that of the energy gap
Δ
2
within the quantum dot, as shown previously in Fig. 5. As a result, Q has maximum values only for low energies and small
Δ
1
. When E increases, Q becomes mostly weak and approaches a constant, regardless of the values taken by
Δ
1
. Another remark is that the behavior of Q decreases as long as
Δ
2
increases. To summarize, the findings in Figs. 5 and 6 show that the presence of an inhomogeneous gap in our system can be used to manipulate resonances. This remains among the interesting conclusions reached right now.

In Fig. 7 , we plot the square modulus of the scattering coefficients |
c
m
|
2
( m = 0 , 1 , 2 , 3 ) as a function of incident energy E to identify the resonances. We used V = 1 ,
Δ
2
= 0.2 , and different quantum dot sizes, with (a):
Δ
1
= 0.1 and (b):
Δ
1
= 0.5 . As a result, we find that the coefficients |
c
m
|
2
strongly vary with quantum dot size, exhibiting resonances. More specifically, the coefficients |
c
m
|
2
show oscillatory behaviors with different amplitudes as long as
r
0
increase. For E <
Δ
1
and small
r
0
, the coefficients |
c
m
|
2
decrease and increase for large
r
0
. However, at the point E =
Δ
1
, one sees that |
c
m
|
2
show minimum peaks. Compared to the results for one energy gap inside the quantum dots [18], we stress that the presence of a gap outside gives a non-null square modulus of the scattering coefficient for both cases E = 0 and E <
Δ
1
. Soon after E =
Δ
1
, the coefficients |
c
m
|
2
clearly exhibit oscillatory behavior.

In Fig. 8 , the radial component of the reflected density current
j
r
r
is plotted as a function of the incident angle θ for V = 1 ,
Δ
2
= 0.2 , and various
Δ
1
values. We notice that
j
r
r
exhibits periodic oscillations with amplitudes that vary with
Δ
1
. As a result,
Δ
1
acts by minimizing and maximizing
j
r
r
in response to different physical parameter choices. In Fig. 8a, there is a single oscillation with a maximum at θ = 0 for the mode m = 0 and three maximum scattering amplitudes for m = 1 as in Fig. 8b. For m = 2 in Fig. 8c there are five maximum scattering amplitudes and seven for m = 3 as in Fig. 8d. We notice that the results are in agreement with those obtained for a circular electrostatically defined quantum dot in gapped graphene [18]. In general, each mode has the same amplitude of the ( 2 m + 1 ) maximum scattering observables, which can be changed by tuning on the
Δ
1
gap outside the quantum dot as schematically depicted in Fig. 1.

We investigated the scattering of Dirac electron in circular graphene quantum dots via an electrostatic potential V with two different gaps
Δ
1
outside and
Δ
2
inside. The energy spectrum solutions are discovered to be dependent on both the gaps
Δ
1
and
Δ
2
. Applying the boundary condition at the interface, the scattering coefficients are explicitly determined. We used the asymptotic behavior of the Hankel function to obtain analytical expressions for the radial component
j
r
r
of current density associated with the reflected wave, the scattering efficiency Q, and the square modulus of the scattering coefficients |
c
m
|
2
by focusing on solutions at large arguments.

Our numerical analysis showed that Q can be manipulated by tuning the gap
Δ
1
outside the quantum dots. More specifically, we discovered that Q decreases as
Δ
1
increases. Furthermore, Q shows oscillatory behavior for some chosen values of
Δ
1
. Among the new discoveries is that
Δ
1
has a significant impact on the behavior of |
c
m
|
2
as a function of incident energy E. In fact, unlike the case of a single energy gap within the quantum dots, |
c
m
|
2
are non-null for two energies, E = 0 and E <
Δ
1
. For the radial component of the reflected current, it was shown that each mode has ( 2 m + 1 ) maximum scattering directions with the same amplitudes that vary by turning on
Δ
1
.

Fatima Belokda: Investigation, Data curation. Ahmed Jellal: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. El Houssine Atmani: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
